question: A patient protrudes their tongue and it deviates toward the right side. Which cranial nerve is most likely affected?
cited_learning_objective: Utilize the neurologic exam to localize lesions including the laterality of a lesion (left/dominant, right/non-dominant), and the location (specific areas of cerebral cortex, deep brain white matter structures, parts of diencephalon, levels of brain stem, cerebellar cortex and related structures, and segments of spinal cord)

options.rationale: Wrong because olfaction is mediated by CN I; damage to CN I would cause anosmia rather than tongue deviation; it would be correct only if the exam showed smell loss instead of tongue deviation.
Wrong because CN II mediates vision and pupillary input; optic nerve lesion causes visual field deficits, not tongue deviation; it would be correct only if visual field loss were present.

Wrong because CN III controls most extraocular muscles and pupil constriction; CN III palsy causes ptosis and eye movement deficits, not tongue deviation; it would be correct only with ophthalmoplegia and pupillary changes.
Wrong because CN IX supplies pharyngeal sensation and parotid salivation; lesion causes absent gag reflex or dysphagia, not tongue deviation; it would be correct only with pharyngeal sensory loss.
Correct because CN XII innervates intrinsic and most extrinsic tongue muscles; unilateral CN XII lesion causes tongue deviation toward the weak side, matching the exam finding.

question: Which reflex is most directly used to assess L4 spinal segment integrity during a bedside exam?
cited_learning_objective: Utilize the neurologic exam to localize lesions including the laterality of a lesion (left/dominant, right/non-dominant), and the location (specific areas of cerebral cortex, deep brain white matter structures, parts of diencephalon, levels of brain stem, cerebellar cortex and related structures, and segments of spinal cord)

options.rationale: Wrong because the biceps reflex primarily tests C5–C6; it would be correct only if the question targeted C5–C6 rather than L4.
Wrong because brachioradialis assesses C6–C7; it would be correct only if mid‑cervical segments were implicated instead of L4.
Correct because the patellar (knee‑jerk) reflex is mediated by L2–L4 nerve roots and is used clinically to assess L4 segment function.
Wrong because the Achilles reflex tests S1; it would be correct only if S1 dysfunction were suspected rather than L4.
Wrong because the plantar response assesses corticospinal tract integrity (UMN signs) rather than a single spinal segment like L4; it would be correct only if looking for Babinski sign.

question: A patient demonstrates dysdiadochokinesia on rapid alternating hand movements. Which structure is most likely dysfunctional?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the presentation of highlighted spinal cord syndromes and recall their likely etiologies.

options.rationale: Wrong because primary motor cortex lesions produce weakness and UMN signs rather than isolated dysdiadochokinesia; it would be correct only if weakness and cortical signs were present.
Wrong because basal ganglia disorders cause bradykinesia or chorea rather than cerebellar dysdiadochokinesia; it would be correct only with resting tremor or choreiform movements.
Correct because lesions of the cerebellar intermediate hemisphere produce ipsilateral appendicular ataxia including dysdiadochokinesia on rapid alternating movements.
Wrong because dorsal column injury causes loss of vibration and proprioception with positive Romberg, not dysdiadochokinesia; it would be correct only with impaired joint position sense.
Wrong because spinothalamic lesions produce pain and temperature loss, not coordination deficits; it would be correct only if analgesia were the primary deficit.

question: Which clinical sign most directly indicates an upper motor neuron lesion on neurologic exam?
cited_learning_objective: Utilize the neurologic exam to localize lesions including the laterality of a lesion (left/dominant, right/non-dominant), and the location (specific areas of cerebral cortex, deep brain white matter structures, parts of diencephalon, levels of brain stem, cerebellar cortex and related structures, and segments of spinal cord)

options.rationale: Wrong because fasciculations are a hallmark of lower motor neuron injury; this would be correct only if LMN pathology were present.
Wrong because pronounced atrophy is typical of LMN lesions; it would be correct only with chronic denervation causing severe wasting.
Correct because UMN lesions produce increased tone, hyperreflexia, and pathological reflexes such as an extensor plantar response (Babinski).
Wrong because absent reflexes suggest LMN or peripheral nerve lesions; it would be correct only if reflex arcs were disrupted.
Wrong because flaccid paralysis is characteristic of LMN lesions; it would be correct only with acute peripheral nerve or ventral horn damage.

question: A patient has contralateral loss of pain and temperature in the body with ipsilateral facial analgesia after a lateral medullary infarct. Which artery is most likely involved?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the presentation of highlighted brainstem syndromes and recall their likely etiologies.

options.rationale: Correct because lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome is classically due to PICA or vertebral artery infarction producing ipsilateral facial analgesia and contralateral body analgesia.
Wrong because AICA lesions produce lateral pontine signs including facial nucleus involvement and Bell palsy; it would be correct only with ipsilateral facial paralysis and cochlear signs.
Wrong because paramedian basilar branches cause medial pontine syndromes with corticospinal involvement; it would be correct only with contralateral hemiparesis sparing face.
Wrong because PCA infarcts typically affect midbrain structures and visual pathways; it would be correct only with occipital lobe or CN III findings.

Wrong because ASA infarction causes medial medullary or anterior cord syndromes with corticospinal and medial lemniscus involvement; it would be correct only with hypoglossal palsy and contralateral hemianesthesia.

question: During exam a patient has absent gag reflex and palate droop with hoarseness. Which cranial nerve pair is most implicated?
cited_learning_objective: Cranial nerve signs in isolation are likely to be caused by peripheral nerve lesions

options.rationale: Correct because CN IX provides pharyngeal sensation and CN X supplies palate elevation and laryngeal function; lesions cause absent gag reflex, palate droop, dysphagia, and hoarseness.
Wrong because CN VII and VIII affect facial expression and hearing/vestibular function; it would be correct only with facial weakness and hearing loss.
Wrong because CN V and VI mediate facial sensation/mastication and lateral gaze; it would be correct only with facial numbness and lateral rectus palsy.
Wrong because CN XI affects SCM/trapezius and CN XII controls tongue; it would be correct only with shoulder weakness and tongue deviation.
Wrong because CN III and IV control extraocular movements and eyelid; it would be correct only with ophthalmoplegia and ptosis.

question: A patient presents with sudden contralateral hemiparesis including lower face and tongue, with minimal cortical sensory signs. Which location is most consistent with these findings?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the presentation of highlighted brainstem syndromes and recall their likely etiologies.

options.rationale: Correct because pure motor hemiparesis often arises from lacunar infarcts in the posterior limb of the internal capsule affecting CST and CBT fibers, producing contralateral weakness including lower face and tongue.
Wrong because cortical lesions typically produce cortical sensory deficits and higher cortical signs like aphasia or neglect; it would be correct only with cortical sensory loss or language deficits.
Wrong because spinal cord lesions cannot explain facial or tongue weakness; it would be correct only if cranial nerve involvement were absent and face spared.

Wrong because medial medullary syndrome includes hypoglossal palsy and contralateral hemianesthesia but often has prominent sensory findings; it would be correct only with ipsilateral tongue deviation plus sensory loss.
Wrong because cerebellar lesions cause ipsilateral ataxia and coordination deficits without hemiparesis; it would be correct only with dysmetria and intention tremor.

question: Which pattern of sensory loss suggests a hemicord lesion at the thoracic level?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize the presentation of highlighted spinal cord syndromes and recall their likely etiologies.

options.text: Ipsilateral vibration loss and contralateral pain loss
options.rationale: Correct because Brown-Séquard (hemicord) causes ipsilateral dorsal column loss (vibration/proprioception) and contralateral spinothalamic loss (pain/temperature) beginning a few levels below the lesion.

options.text: Bilateral loss of all modalities below lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because complete transverse cord lesions produce bilateral loss of all modalities; it would be correct only with a full transection rather than a hemicord lesion.
Wrong because isolated segmental analgesia suggests peripheral nerve or dorsal horn lesion; it would be correct only if the lesion were limited to dorsal horn or root.

options.text: Sacral sparing with diffuse upper loss
options.rationale: Wrong because sacral sparing is typical of central cord syndrome where sacral fibers are spared; it would be correct only with central cord pathology.

options.text: Ipsilateral pain loss and contralateral vibration loss
options.rationale: Wrong because this reverses the expected pattern for hemicord lesions; it would be correct only if the lesion affected tracts in an atypical or crossed manner.

question: A patient has a high-frequency tremor that worsens with sustained posture and improves with a small amount of alcohol. Which diagnosis fits best?

options.rationale: Correct because essential tremor is a high-frequency postural/action tremor that often has familial tendency and characteristically improves transiently with alcohol.
Wrong because Parkinsonian tremor is a low-frequency resting tremor that improves with voluntary movement; it would be correct only with resting tremor and bradykinesia.
Wrong because cerebellar intention tremor is low-frequency and occurs with goal‑directed movement, not with sustained posture; it would be correct only with dysmetria on reaching.
Wrong because hemiballismus is violent flinging movements due to subthalamic nucleus lesions, not a high-frequency postural tremor; it would be correct only with flinging limb movements.

Wrong because dystonic tremor is associated with sustained muscle contractions and abnormal postures rather than an isolated high-frequency postural tremor improved by alcohol; it would be correct only with dystonic posturing.

question: Which clinical finding would most strongly suggest a cerebellar vermis lesion rather than a cerebellar hemisphere lesion?

options.rationale: Correct because vermis lesions classically cause truncal ataxia with wide‑based, unsteady gait and difficulty maintaining posture, distinguishing them from appendicular hemisphere findings.

options.text: Ipsilateral limb dysmetria on finger‑nose testing
options.rationale: Wrong because limb dysmetria is typical of cerebellar hemisphere lesions affecting appendicular coordination; it would be correct only with limb coordination deficits.
Wrong because intention tremor is associated with hemisphere lesions affecting limb movements; it would be correct only with goal‑directed movement impairment.
Wrong because vertical gaze palsy implicates midbrain structures rather than the cerebellar vermis; it would be correct only with midbrain involvement.

options.text: Positive Romberg with eyes closed only
options.rationale: Wrong because Romberg sign with eyes closed suggests dorsal column sensory ataxia rather than cerebellar vermis dysfunction; it would be correct only with proprioceptive loss.

question: A patient presents with sudden facial paralysis involving the entire ipsilateral face including forehead. Which lesion location is most consistent with this exam?
cited_learning_objective: Cranial nerve signs in isolation are likely to be caused by peripheral nerve lesions

options.rationale: Correct because a peripheral CN VII lesion (Bell palsy) produces ipsilateral weakness of both upper and lower face, including forehead, distinguishing it from supranuclear lesions.
Wrong because supranuclear (UMN) lesions typically spare the forehead due to bilateral cortical innervation; it would be correct only with contralateral lower face weakness sparing forehead.
Wrong because a facial nucleus lesion in the pons can mimic peripheral palsy but often includes additional pontine signs; it would be correct only with accompanying brainstem findings.
Wrong because cortical temporal lobe lesions rarely produce isolated full‑face paralysis; it would be correct only with extensive cortical motor strip involvement.
Wrong because CN XI affects SCM and trapezius causing shoulder and head rotation deficits, not facial paralysis; it would be correct only with shoulder weakness.

question: Which exam finding best differentiates a dorsal column lesion from a spinothalamic tract lesion?
cited_learning_objective: Deficits associated with DC-ML pathway damage

options.text: Loss of vibration and joint position sense
options.rationale: Correct because dorsal column (DC‑ML) lesions produce loss of conscious proprioception and vibration, often with positive Romberg, distinguishing them from spinothalamic lesions.

options.text: Loss of pain and temperature sensation
options.rationale: Wrong because loss of pain and temperature is characteristic of spinothalamic tract lesions, not dorsal column injury; it would be correct only with analgesia and thermoanesthesia.
Wrong because ipsilateral facial analgesia implicates trigeminal or lateral brainstem involvement rather than dorsal columns; it would be correct only with trigeminal nucleus lesion.
Wrong because Babinski indicates corticospinal tract (UMN) dysfunction rather than a primary sensory tract lesion; it would be correct only with motor pathway involvement.
Wrong because absent corneal reflex implicates trigeminal or facial nerve dysfunction, not dorsal column pathways; it would be correct only with cranial nerve lesions.

question: A patient has sudden onset vertigo, nystagmus, ipsilateral limb ataxia, and loss of facial pain sensation with contralateral body analgesia. Which brainstem syndrome is most consistent?
cited_learning_objective: The Neurologic Exam Applied to Brainstem Syndromes

options.rationale: Correct because Wallenberg (lateral medullary) syndrome produces vestibular symptoms, ipsilateral cerebellar signs, ipsilateral facial analgesia, and contralateral body pain/temperature loss due to PICA/vertebral infarct.

Wrong because medial medullary syndrome features hypoglossal palsy and contralateral hemianesthesia without dense ipsilateral facial analgesia; it would be correct only with tongue deviation and corticospinal signs.
Wrong because lateral pontine (AICA) lesions often include facial nucleus palsy and cochlear signs; it would be correct only with ipsilateral facial paralysis and hearing loss.
Wrong because medial pontine lesions produce CN VI palsy and contralateral hemiparesis sparing face; it would be correct only with abducens palsy and corticospinal signs.

Wrong because medial midbrain lesions cause CN III palsy and contralateral hemiparesis with possible ataxia, not the pattern of vestibular and facial sensory loss described; it would be correct only with oculomotor palsy.

question: Which combination of exam findings would most strongly localize a lesion to the posterior limb of the internal capsule?

options.text: Contralateral pure motor weakness with hyperreflexia
options.rationale: Correct because lesions of the posterior limb of the internal capsule (lacunar infarct) disrupt CST/CBT fibers producing contralateral spastic weakness with hyperreflexia and minimal cortical signs.
Wrong because ipsilateral facial numbness and ataxia suggest lateral brainstem or cerebellar peduncle lesions, not internal capsule; it would be correct only with those brainstem findings.

options.text: Bilateral lower extremity weakness with urinary retention
options.rationale: Wrong because bilateral leg weakness and sphincter dysfunction point to spinal cord or ACA watershed lesions, not a unilateral internal capsule lesion; it would be correct only with spinal cord involvement.

options.text: Contralateral visual field cut with aphasia
options.rationale: Wrong because visual field deficits and aphasia implicate cortical or optic radiation involvement (PCA/MCA territory), not isolated internal capsule lesions; it would be correct only with cortical extension.
Wrong because flaccid paralysis and hyporeflexia indicate LMN or peripheral nerve lesions rather than an internal capsule UMN lesion; it would be correct only with peripheral nerve damage.

question: A patient demonstrates a 'cape' distribution loss of pain and temperature across shoulders and upper torso with preserved fine touch. Which spinal pathology best explains this?

options.text: Small central cord lesion at cervical levels
options.rationale: Correct because small central cord lesions (syringomyelia or contusion) damage decussating spinothalamic fibers producing bilateral 'cape' or 'suspended' analgesia with preserved dorsal column modalities.
Wrong because hemicord (Brown‑Séquard) produces ipsilateral dorsal column loss and contralateral pain loss, not a symmetric cape distribution; it would be correct only with asymmetric findings.

options.text: Anterior cord infarction at thoracic level
options.rationale: Wrong because anterior cord syndrome causes bilateral motor and pain loss below lesion with variable dorsal column sparing, not a cape distribution; it would be correct only with motor deficits.
Wrong because peripheral neuropathy produces distal glove‑stock sensory loss rather than a symmetric cape across shoulders and torso; it would be correct only with distal sensory deficits.

options.text: Dorsal column degeneration in lumbar cord
options.rationale: Wrong because dorsal column degeneration causes vibration and proprioception loss in lower limbs with Romberg sign, not bilateral upper torso analgesia; it would be correct only with proprioceptive deficits.

question: Which clinical constellation most strongly suggests a lesion of the lateral pons due to AICA infarction?

options.text: Ipsilateral facial paralysis with hearing loss
options.rationale: Correct because AICA (lateral pontine) lesions often involve the facial nucleus producing Bell palsy and the cochlear nuclei causing sensorineural hearing loss, along with ipsilateral analgesia and ataxia.
Wrong because contralateral hemiparesis sparing face is more typical of medial pontine or corticospinal involvement, not lateral AICA lesions; it would be correct only with medial pontine infarct.

options.text: Ipsilateral tongue deviation with contralateral anesthesia
options.rationale: Wrong because tongue deviation implicates hypoglossal nucleus in medial medulla, not lateral pons; it would be correct only with medial medullary involvement.

options.text: Bilateral vertical gaze palsy and coma
options.rationale: Wrong because vertical gaze palsy and impaired consciousness suggest midbrain or tectal lesions, not lateral pontine infarction; it would be correct only with midbrain damage.

options.text: Isolated ataxia without cranial nerve signs
options.rationale: Wrong because isolated ataxia without cranial nerve findings suggests cerebellar hemisphere lesion rather than lateral pontine syndrome; it would be correct only with pure cerebellar pathology.

question: A patient has contralateral loss of vibration and proprioception with ipsilateral tongue weakness and contralateral spasticity. Which brainstem syndrome fits this pattern?

options.rationale: Correct because medial medullary syndrome involves the medial lemniscus (contralateral proprioception loss), CN XII (ipsilateral tongue weakness), and corticospinal tract (contralateral UMN signs).

Wrong because lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome produces ipsilateral facial analgesia and contralateral body analgesia with vestibular signs, not hypoglossal palsy; it would be correct only with those lateral features.
Wrong because medial pontine lesions affect CN VI and corticospinal tracts but not CN XII or medial lemniscus in this pattern; it would be correct only with abducens palsy and corticospinal signs.
Wrong because medial midbrain lesions involve CN III and red nucleus with different sensory patterns; it would be correct only with oculomotor palsy and contralateral ataxia.

Wrong because central pontine myelinolysis causes bilateral corticospinal and corticobulbar dysfunction (e.g., locked‑in features) rather than the alternating hypoglossal hemiplegia described; it would be correct only with symmetric pontine involvement.

question: Which exam finding would most specifically indicate dorsal column dysfunction at the level of the spinal cord?
cited_learning_objective: Deficits associated with DC-ML pathway damage

options.text: Loss of joint position sense in toes
options.rationale: Correct because dorsal column lesions produce loss of conscious proprioception and vibration, commonly tested at the big toe and thumb, leading to impaired joint position sense.

options.text: Loss of pinprick sensation in limbs
options.rationale: Wrong because pinprick tests spinothalamic (pain) pathways, not dorsal columns; it would be correct only with spinothalamic tract injury.
Wrong because corneal reflex involves trigeminal afferent and facial efferent cranial nerves, not dorsal columns; it would be correct only with cranial nerve lesions.
Wrong because hyperreflexia and clonus indicate corticospinal (UMN) involvement rather than isolated dorsal column dysfunction; it would be correct only with UMN tract damage.
Wrong because two‑point discrimination on the face implicates trigeminal pathways and cortical sensory areas, not spinal dorsal columns; it would be correct only with trigeminal or cortical lesions.

question: A patient with progressive weakness has both flaccid limbs with fasciculations and later develops spasticity. Which disease process from the lecture best matches this course?

options.rationale: Correct because ALS causes combined degeneration of LMNs (flaccid weakness, fasciculations) and UMNs (spasticity) in cortex, brainstem, and spinal cord without primary sensory loss.
Wrong because poliomyelitis selectively destroys LMNs causing flaccid paralysis without progressive UMN spasticity; it would be correct only with isolated LMN signs post‑infection.

Wrong because subacute combined degeneration produces dorsal column and corticospinal tract degeneration with sensory loss and UMN signs, not initial LMN fasciculations; it would be correct only with prominent proprioceptive loss.
Wrong because diabetic neuropathy causes distal symmetric sensory loss and LMN features, not combined progressive UMN signs; it would be correct only with distal sensory‑predominant findings.

Wrong because transverse myelitis causes bilateral motor and sensory deficits below the lesion with acute progression, not the LMN‑to‑UMN evolution described; it would be correct only with a spinal cord sensory level.

question: Which bedside test best distinguishes cerebellar ataxia from sensory ataxia due to dorsal column loss?

options.rationale: Correct because a positive Romberg (worse with eyes closed) indicates sensory ataxia from dorsal column dysfunction, whereas cerebellar ataxia persists with eyes open and closed.
Wrong because finger‑nose‑finger assesses cerebellar dysmetria but does not reliably distinguish sensory from cerebellar ataxia; it would be correct only to demonstrate cerebellar involvement.
Wrong because heel‑shin evaluates cerebellar coordination of the lower limb, not specifically dorsal column function; it would be correct only with cerebellar deficits.

Wrong because two‑point discrimination assesses cortical sensory discrimination and dorsal column integrity but does not directly compare gait stability with vision; it would be correct only with cortical sensory loss.

options.text: Observation of resting tremor at rest
options.rationale: Wrong because resting tremor suggests basal ganglia dysfunction (Parkinsonism), not a test to differentiate cerebellar versus sensory ataxia; it would be correct only with Parkinsonian features.

question: A patient with a unilateral lesion of the cerebellar hemisphere will most likely show which lateralizing sign on exam?

options.rationale: Correct because cerebellar hemisphere lesions produce ipsilateral appendicular ataxia, including dysmetria and intention tremor on the same side as the lesion.
Wrong because hemiparesis with hyperreflexia indicates corticospinal tract involvement, not isolated cerebellar hemisphere lesions; it would be correct only with CST damage.

options.text: Bilateral truncal ataxia and wide gait
options.rationale: Wrong because truncal ataxia and wide‑based gait are typical of vermis lesions rather than unilateral hemisphere lesions; it would be correct only with midline vermis involvement.

options.text: Loss of pain and temperature on face
options.rationale: Wrong because facial analgesia implicates trigeminal or lateral brainstem pathways, not cerebellar hemisphere; it would be correct only with trigeminal nucleus lesion.

options.text: Positive Romberg only with eyes open
options.rationale: Wrong because Romberg positivity with eyes open suggests severe truncal instability or posterior vermis lesion, not a unilateral cerebellar hemisphere lesion; it would be correct only with midline dysfunction.

question: A patient presents with sudden inability to abduct the left eye and contralateral hemiparesis sparing the face. Which pontine syndrome is most consistent?

options.rationale: Correct because medial pontine lesions affect the abducens nucleus (CN VI) causing ipsilateral lateral gaze palsy and corticospinal fibers producing contralateral hemiparesis that may spare the face.

Wrong because lateral pontine (AICA) lesions produce facial nucleus signs and ipsilateral facial paralysis rather than isolated abducens palsy with contralateral hemiparesis; it would be correct only with facial paralysis and hearing loss.
Wrong because medial medullary syndrome involves hypoglossal dysfunction and medial lemniscus, not CN VI; it would be correct only with tongue deviation and contralateral sensory loss.

Wrong because locked‑in results from bilateral ventral pontine lesions causing quadriplegia and preserved vertical eye movements, not a unilateral abducens palsy with contralateral hemiparesis; it would be correct only with bilateral ventral pontine damage.

Wrong because lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome causes vestibular and sensory deficits with dysphagia, not abducens palsy and contralateral hemiparesis; it would be correct only with PICA territory signs.

question: A patient with a unilateral lesion of the corticobulbar tract above the pons demonstrates contralateral lower facial weakness but preserved forehead movement. Which explanation fits this finding?

options.rationale: Correct because corticobulbar fibers to upper facial nucleus are bilateral, so supranuclear lesions produce contralateral lower face weakness while forehead muscles remain spared due to bilateral cortical input.

options.text: Peripheral facial nerve lesion affecting entire face
options.rationale: Wrong because peripheral CN VII lesions cause ipsilateral weakness of both upper and lower face including forehead; it would be correct only with LMN facial nerve involvement.

options.text: Lesion of facial nucleus in pons
options.rationale: Wrong because facial nucleus lesions produce ipsilateral full‑face paralysis including forehead; it would be correct only with pontine nucleus damage.
Wrong because hypoglossal nucleus affects tongue movement, not facial expression; it would be correct only with tongue deviation and dysarthria.
Wrong because corticospinal lesions produce limb UMN signs rather than isolated lower facial weakness; it would be correct only with widespread motor deficits.

question: A patient has sudden onset dysphagia, hoarseness, and an ipsilateral loss of facial pain with contralateral body analgesia after a brainstem stroke. Which nucleus involvement explains the absent gag reflex?

options.rationale: Correct because nucleus ambiguus (CN IX/X motor) supplies pharyngeal and laryngeal muscles; its lesion causes palate droop, dysphagia, hoarseness, and absent gag reflex as seen in lateral medullary or jugular foramen lesions.
Wrong because hypoglossal nucleus (CN XII) controls tongue muscles and would cause tongue deviation, not absent gag reflex; it would be correct only with tongue weakness.
Wrong because facial nucleus (CN VII) affects facial expression and stapedius, not pharyngeal elevation or gag reflex; it would be correct only with facial paralysis.
Wrong because trigeminal motor nucleus controls mastication muscles and corneal reflex afferent, not palate elevation; it would be correct only with jaw weakness and facial sensory loss.
Wrong because dorsal motor nucleus of vagus mediates parasympathetic output to thoracoabdominal viscera, not gag reflex motor function; it would be correct only with autonomic dysfunction.

question: A patient with a lesion affecting the posterior limb of the internal capsule is most likely to present with which combination of signs on the neurologic exam?

options.text: Contralateral spastic weakness with facial involvement
options.rationale: Correct because posterior limb internal capsule lesions disrupt corticospinal and corticobulbar fibers producing contralateral spastic paresis including lower face and tongue involvement with hyperreflexia.
Wrong because isolated ipsilateral sensory loss suggests peripheral nerve or dorsal column lesion, not an internal capsule lesion; it would be correct only with peripheral or dorsal column pathology.
Wrong because cerebellar ataxia without weakness indicates cerebellar pathology, not internal capsule CST disruption; it would be correct only with cerebellar lesions.
Wrong because flaccid paralysis and hyporeflexia indicate LMN or peripheral nerve lesions rather than UMN internal capsule lesions; it would be correct only with peripheral nerve damage.
Wrong because isolated visual field defects implicate optic radiations or occipital cortex, not internal capsule lesions; it would be correct only with PCA territory involvement.

question: A patient presents with progressive numbness and loss of vibration in the lower extremities, a positive Romberg, and high‑stepping gait. Which diagnosis from the lecture best fits this presentation?

options.rationale: Correct because tabes dorsalis involves degeneration of dorsal roots and dorsal columns causing impaired vibration and proprioception, positive Romberg, high‑stepping ataxic gait, and painful paresthesias.
Wrong because ALS produces combined LMN and UMN motor signs without primary sensory loss or Romberg positivity; it would be correct only with progressive motor weakness and fasciculations.

Wrong because subacute combined degeneration causes dorsal column and corticospinal involvement with sensory loss and UMN signs, but is typically due to B12 deficiency rather than neurosyphilis; it would be correct only with B12 deficiency evidence.

Wrong because diabetic neuropathy causes distal symmetric sensory loss and may cause gait disturbance but usually lacks the classic high‑stepping gait and Romberg of tabes dorsalis; it would be correct only with metabolic risk factors.

Wrong because syringomyelia produces a cape‑like loss of pain and temperature with preserved vibration early, not the dorsal column degeneration and high‑stepping gait of tabes dorsalis; it would be correct only with central cord signs.

question: A patient with a unilateral lesion of the spinothalamic tract at the level of C5 will most likely have which sensory deficit pattern?
cited_learning_objective: INJURY TO THE LATERAL SPINOTHALAMIC TRACT (LSTT)

options.text: Contralateral loss of pain below C5
options.rationale: Correct because spinothalamic fibers cross near their entry level and ascend contralaterally; a unilateral lesion at C5 produces contralateral analgesia and thermoanesthesia beginning a few segments below the lesion.

options.text: Ipsilateral loss of vibration below C5
options.rationale: Wrong because vibration/proprioception are dorsal column modalities and would be ipsilateral only with dorsal column lesions; it would be correct only with dorsal column involvement.

options.text: Bilateral loss of all modalities at C5
options.rationale: Wrong because bilateral loss of all modalities suggests a transverse cord lesion rather than a unilateral spinothalamic lesion; it would be correct only with complete cord transection.

options.text: Ipsilateral facial analgesia and contralateral body analgesia
options.rationale: Wrong because this alternating pattern is characteristic of lateral brainstem lesions (e.g., lateral medulla), not a unilateral cervical spinothalamic tract lesion; it would be correct only with brainstem involvement.
Wrong because two‑point discrimination is a cortical/dorsal column function and would not be isolated with a spinothalamic lesion; it would be correct only with cortical or dorsal column damage.

question: A patient with a lesion of the anterior spinal artery at T8 presents acutely with bilateral loss of pain and temperature below the lesion and flaccid weakness at the level. Which additional finding is most likely?

options.text: Preserved vibration and proprioception below lesion
options.rationale: Correct because anterior spinal artery infarction typically spares the dorsal columns, so vibration and joint position sense are often preserved while motor and spinothalamic modalities are lost.

options.text: Bilateral loss of vibration below lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because dorsal column loss would be expected only if the posterior columns were involved; it would be correct only with a more extensive posterior infarct.
Wrong because facial sensory loss implicates trigeminal or brainstem lesions, not a thoracic anterior spinal artery infarct; it would be correct only with brainstem involvement.
Wrong because a T8 lesion affects trunk and lower limbs rather than isolated upper limbs; it would be correct only with cervical cord involvement.

options.text: Hyperreflexia at level with preserved strength
options.rationale: Wrong because acute anterior cord lesions often produce flaccid weakness at the level (LMN signs) with later spasticity below; hyperreflexia with preserved strength would be atypical early on.

question: A patient has progressive bilateral spasticity and sensory loss below a thoracic lesion after trauma. The sacral segments are relatively spared. Which syndrome best matches this evolution?

options.rationale: Correct because large central cord lesions produce bilateral spastic paralysis and analgesia below the lesion with relative sacral sparing since sacral fibers are most superficial and often spared initially.
Wrong because complete transection causes total bilateral loss of motor and sensory function below the lesion without sacral sparing; it would be correct only with full cord severance.
Wrong because hemicord lesions produce ipsilateral motor and dorsal column loss with contralateral pain loss, not symmetric bilateral spasticity; it would be correct only with unilateral findings.

Wrong because anterior cord syndrome primarily affects motor and pain modalities but may not spare sacral segments in the same pattern as central cord lesions; it would be correct only with ASA infarction features.
Wrong because peripheral polyneuropathy causes distal symmetric sensory and motor deficits rather than a spinal level with sacral sparing; it would be correct only with peripheral nerve distribution.

question: A patient with a lesion of the lateral corticospinal tract in the cervical spinal cord will most likely show which pattern of motor signs?

options.rationale: Correct because LCST fibers have already decussated at the medullary pyramids, so a lesion in the spinal cord produces ipsilateral UMN signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia) below the level.
Wrong because contralateral deficits would occur with supraspinal lesions before decussation; it would be correct only with a brainstem or cortical lesion above the decussation.

options.text: Bilateral LMN weakness at all levels
options.rationale: Wrong because bilateral LMN weakness across all levels suggests diffuse peripheral neuropathy or anterior horn disease, not a focal LCST lesion; it would be correct only with widespread LMN pathology.

options.text: Isolated facial weakness without limb signs
options.rationale: Wrong because spinal LCST lesions do not affect cranial nerve nuclei and cannot explain isolated facial weakness; it would be correct only with brainstem or cortical involvement.
Wrong because UMN spasticity typically resists passive movement and does not improve strength; it would be correct only in non‑neurologic weakness.

question: A patient presents with sudden vertigo, nystagmus, ipsilateral facial numbness, and contralateral body analgesia after a posterior inferior cerebellar artery occlusion. Which additional sign would you expect on the ipsilateral side?

options.rationale: Correct because lateral medullary (PICA) lesions can disrupt descending sympathetic fibers producing ipsilateral ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis (Horner syndrome) along with the described sensory and vestibular signs.
Wrong because facial paralysis is more typical of facial nucleus or lateral pontine lesions (AICA), not lateral medullary infarcts; it would be correct only with facial nucleus involvement.
Wrong because bilateral vibration loss suggests dorsal column involvement, not lateral medullary lesions; it would be correct only with medial lemniscus or dorsal column damage.
Wrong because tongue deviation implicates hypoglossal nucleus in medial medullary syndrome, not lateral medulla; it would be correct only with medial medullary involvement.
Wrong because bilateral lower extremity UMN signs suggest more rostral corticospinal involvement or spinal cord pathology, not a lateral medullary infarct; it would be correct only with CST damage.

question: A patient with a lesion of the medial lemniscus in the brainstem will most likely have which sensory deficit pattern?

options.text: Contralateral loss of vibration and proprioception
options.rationale: Correct because the medial lemniscus carries dorsal column modalities after decussation; brainstem lesions of the medial lemniscus produce contralateral loss of vibration and joint position sense.

options.text: Ipsilateral loss of pain and temperature
options.rationale: Wrong because pain and temperature are carried by the spinothalamic tract, not the medial lemniscus; it would be correct only with spinothalamic or dorsal horn involvement.
Wrong because bilateral facial analgesia implicates trigeminal nucleus or bilateral brainstem lesions, not a unilateral medial lemniscus lesion; it would be correct only with trigeminal involvement.
Wrong because anosmia is due to olfactory bulb/nerve lesions, not medial lemniscus damage; it would be correct only with olfactory pathway injury.

options.text: Loss of two‑point discrimination on ipsilateral hand
options.rationale: Wrong because two‑point discrimination is a cortical function and would be contralateral with medial lemniscus lesions; it would be correct only with ipsilateral cortical or peripheral nerve pathology.

question: A patient presents with sudden flaccid paralysis of lower extremities after anterior spinal artery thrombosis at T12. Which autonomic dysfunction is most likely to occur acutely?

options.rationale: Correct because anterior cord lesions disrupt descending autonomic pathways controlling bladder function; lesions at or within sacral segments often produce flaccid bladder and urinary retention acutely.
Wrong because spinal cord lesions more commonly cause urinary retention and constipation initially; hyperactive bowel movements would be atypical and suggest enteric pathology.
Wrong because salivation is mediated by cranial parasympathetic nuclei, not thoracolumbar spinal autonomics; it would be correct only with brainstem or cranial nerve involvement.

Wrong because priapism can occur with spinal cord injury but preserved bladder control is unlikely with an anterior cord lesion at T12; it would be correct only with selective autonomic pathway sparing.
Wrong because localized hyperhidrosis is not the typical acute autonomic presentation of anterior cord infarction; it would be correct only with specific sympathetic overactivity.

question: A patient has progressive weakness with fasciculations, preserved sensation, and both UMN and LMN signs on exam. Which diagnostic feature from the lecture distinguishes this disease from pure LMN disorders?

options.text: Combination of UMN and LMN signs
options.rationale: Correct because ALS uniquely involves both upper motor neuron degeneration (spasticity, hyperreflexia) and lower motor neuron degeneration (fasciculations, atrophy) while typically sparing primary sensation.

options.text: Isolated anterior horn cell loss only
options.rationale: Wrong because isolated anterior horn cell loss (e.g., poliomyelitis) produces LMN signs without UMN features; it would be correct only with pure LMN presentation.
Wrong because ALS spares sensory modalities; predominant sensory neuropathy would indicate peripheral neuropathy rather than ALS and would be correct only with sensory deficits.
Wrong because ALS is a progressive neurodegenerative disease without response to antivirals; it would be correct only if the condition were infectious and treatable.

options.text: Marked autonomic failure early in course
options.rationale: Wrong because early autonomic failure is not characteristic of ALS; it would be correct only with diseases primarily affecting autonomic pathways.

question: A patient with a unilateral lesion of the cerebellar flocculus most commonly presents with which ocular finding?

options.rationale: Correct because the flocculus is involved in gaze holding and vestibulo‑ocular control; lesions produce gaze‑evoked nystagmus and other gaze dysfunctions on the ipsilateral side.
Wrong because ptosis and pupillary dilation implicate oculomotor nerve or sympathetic pathway lesions, not the cerebellar flocculus; it would be correct only with CN III or sympathetic involvement.
Wrong because corneal reflex involves trigeminal afferent and facial efferent pathways, not the flocculus; it would be correct only with trigeminal or facial nerve lesions.
Wrong because homonymous hemianopia results from optic tract or occipital lobe lesions, not cerebellar flocculus dysfunction; it would be correct only with visual pathway damage.
Wrong because vertical gaze palsy implicates midbrain structures (e.g., rostral interstitial nucleus), not the flocculus; it would be correct only with midbrain lesions.

question: A patient presents with sudden onset of contralateral hemiparesis including lower face and tongue, and ipsilateral oculomotor palsy. Which vascular territory is most likely involved?

options.rationale: Correct because medial midbrain syndrome from paramedian PCA branches can affect CN III (oculomotor) and adjacent corticospinal/corticobulbar fibers producing ipsilateral oculomotor palsy with contralateral hemiparesis.
Wrong because AICA supplies lateral pons producing facial nucleus and cochlear signs, not CN III palsy with contralateral hemiparesis; it would be correct only with lateral pontine features.
Wrong because PICA causes lateral medullary syndrome with vestibular and sensory findings, not oculomotor palsy; it would be correct only with Wallenberg features.
Wrong because paramedian basilar branches affect the pons producing CN VI and corticospinal signs, not CN III; it would be correct only with pontine signs.
Wrong because superior MCA infarcts produce cortical motor and language deficits but not isolated CN III palsy; it would be correct only with cortical signs like aphasia.

question: A patient with a penetrating trauma to the left hemicord at T10 develops ipsilateral spastic paralysis below the lesion and contralateral loss of pain and temperature beginning a few segments below. Which mechanism explains the contralateral analgesia?

options.rationale: Correct because spinothalamic fibers cross within a few segments of entry via the anterior white commissure; a hemicord lesion interrupts these crossed fibers producing contralateral analgesia below the lesion.
Wrong because dorsal column damage causes ipsilateral loss of vibration and proprioception, not contralateral pain loss; it would be correct only with ipsilateral proprioceptive deficits.
Wrong because bilateral CST lesions produce bilateral motor deficits rather than the ipsilateral spasticity and contralateral analgesia pattern; it would be correct only with bilateral motor signs.
Wrong because ASA compression causes bilateral motor and pain loss below the lesion, not the dissociated hemicord pattern; it would be correct only with complete anterior cord ischemia.

Wrong because peripheral nerve injury produces ipsilateral dermatomal sensory loss and LMN signs, not the crossed analgesia seen in hemicord lesions; it would be correct only with peripheral distribution deficits.

question: A patient with a lesion of the vestibulocochlear nerve on the left will most likely present with which combination of signs?

options.rationale: Correct because CN VIII conveys hearing and vestibular information; lesions produce ipsilateral sensorineural hearing loss and vestibular symptoms such as vertigo and nystagmus.
Wrong because facial paralysis and hyperacusis are features of CN VII lesions; they would be correct only with facial nerve involvement.
Wrong because vibration sense is mediated by dorsal columns and would not be affected by CN VIII lesions; it would be correct only with dorsal column or central lesions.
Wrong because gag reflex and swallowing involve CN IX/X, not CN VIII; it would be correct only with nucleus ambiguus or vagal involvement.
Wrong because anosmia and taste disturbances implicate CN I and CN VII/IX respectively, not CN VIII; it would be correct only with olfactory or gustatory nerve lesions.

question: A patient with a lesion of the corticospinal tract at the level of the cerebral peduncle will most likely have which laterality of motor deficits?

options.rationale: Correct because corticospinal fibers above the decussation (in the brain) produce contralateral motor deficits; a lesion in the cerebral peduncle yields contralateral hemiparesis.
Wrong because ipsilateral deficits occur with spinal LCST lesions after decussation; it would be correct only with a spinal cord lesion below the pyramidal decussation.
Wrong because bilateral symmetric weakness suggests bilateral or midline lesions such as locked‑in syndrome, not a unilateral peduncular lesion; it would be correct only with bilateral involvement.

Wrong because isolated facial weakness without limb involvement suggests corticobulbar or peripheral facial nerve lesions, not a corticospinal peduncle lesion; it would be correct only with corticobulbar involvement.
Wrong because acute UMN lesions may transiently be flaccid but classically produce spasticity and hyperreflexia; persistent flaccidity with absent reflexes indicates LMN pathology.

question: A patient presents with sudden onset of ipsilateral facial paralysis, ipsilateral hearing loss, vertigo, and contralateral body analgesia. Which vascular lesion best explains this cluster?

options.rationale: Correct because AICA (lateral pontine) infarcts commonly involve CN VII (facial paralysis), CN VIII (hearing/vestibular signs), cerebellar peduncles (ataxia), and spinal lemniscus (contralateral analgesia).

Wrong because PICA lesions produce lateral medullary signs (dysphagia, hoarseness) and vestibular symptoms but typically spare facial nucleus and cochlear structures; it would be correct only with medullary features.

Wrong because paramedian basilar branches cause medial pontine syndromes with abducens and corticospinal involvement, not the facial and cochlear signs described; it would be correct only with medial pontine signs.

Wrong because PCA occlusion affects midbrain and occipital structures producing visual field defects and CN III palsy, not the combination of facial paralysis and hearing loss; it would be correct only with visual or oculomotor findings.

Wrong because ASA thrombosis affects medial medulla or spinal cord producing motor and pain deficits, not cranial nerve VII/VIII signs; it would be correct only with hypoglossal or spinal cord findings.

question: A patient with a lesion of the dorsal columns at the level of the medulla will most likely have which combination of deficits?

options.text: Contralateral loss of vibration and proprioception
options.rationale: Correct because dorsal column fibers decussate in the caudal medulla; a lesion of the medial lemniscus or decussating dorsal columns at the medulla produces contralateral loss of vibration and joint position sense.

options.text: Ipsilateral loss of pain and temperature
options.rationale: Wrong because pain and temperature are carried by the spinothalamic tract and would not be lost ipsilaterally from a dorsal column lesion; it would be correct only with spinothalamic involvement.
Wrong because facial paralysis implicates facial nucleus or nerve, not dorsal columns; it would be correct only with facial nucleus or peripheral nerve damage.
Wrong because anosmia and taste disturbances involve olfactory and gustatory pathways, not dorsal columns; it would be correct only with cranial nerve I or VII/IX lesions.

options.text: Loss of corneal reflex with jaw deviation
options.rationale: Wrong because corneal reflex and jaw deviation involve trigeminal and facial motor pathways, not dorsal column function; it would be correct only with trigeminal or facial nucleus lesions.

question: A patient with a lesion of the accessory nerve on the right will most likely demonstrate which physical sign?
cited_learning_objective: IX: Glossopharyngeal; X: Vagus; XI: Accessory; XII: Hypoglossal (Cranial Nerves Summary)

options.text: Weakness turning head to left side
options.rationale: Correct because the accessory nerve (CN XI) innervates the sternocleidomastoid which turns the head to the opposite side; a right CN XI lesion causes weakness when turning the head to the left.
Wrong because tongue deviation is due to hypoglossal (CN XII) lesions, not accessory nerve damage; it would be correct only with CN XII involvement.
Wrong because gag reflex involves CN IX and X, not CN XI; it would be correct only with nucleus ambiguus or vagal involvement.
Wrong because facial droop implicates CN VII peripheral lesions; it would be correct only with facial nerve or nucleus damage.

Wrong because bilateral shoulder elevation weakness would require bilateral trapezius (CN XI) lesions; a unilateral right CN XI lesion produces ipsilateral shoulder droop rather than bilateral weakness.
